title: Per-Olof Wikstrom and Robert J. Sampson (Eds): The Explanation of Crime. Context, Mechanisms and Development.
abstract: The criminological discourse is (still) characterized by the co-existence of competing paradigms without much convergence for some decades (see Imhof and Mehlkop, 2007: 151). Well-known examples for approaches operating strictly either on the macro level or on the micro level are the theory of Anomie (Merton, 1968) and the Rational Choice approach (Becker, 1968). But methodological purity has not yet delivered satisfying results (as argued by Bunge in his chapter in this volume), so today a lot of effort is put into the integration of different theories, methods, levels of analysis, and disciplines. But the road to integration is winding, full of rocks, roundabouts, and sometimes leading into a dead end. The seven essays in this volume edited by Per-Olof H. Wikstöm and Robert J. Sampson definitely deliver a highly needed road-map. The innovative, stimulating, and often provocative chapters are organized around the fundamental (interrelated) concepts in the understanding of crime: social context, individual development, and causal mechanisms.
